content.problemStatement:
There are NNN different types of coins.

The iii-th type of coin is worth AiA_iAi​ dollars, and you have BiB_iBi​ coins of this type with you.

There is an item with cost XXX that you'd like to buy. What's the minimum number of **distinct types** of coins you need to use to buy it? **You are allowed to pay more than XXX to buy the item: you need not pay exactly XXX.**

If it's not possible to buy the item no matter what, print −1-1−1.

**Note:**


The objective is to minimize the number of distinct types of coins used, which is not the same thing as minimizing the number of coins used.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and XXX — the number of types of coins and the price of the item, respectively.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots , A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the values of the coins.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1,B_2,\ldots , B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​ — the number of each type of coin with you.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line a single integer: the minimum number of distinct types of coins needed to buy the item, or −1-1−1 if it's impossible to buy the item.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 10510^5105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Choose all coins of type 333 and one coin of type 222, to obtain a value of 3+3+3+2=113+3+3+2 = 113+3+3+2=11 using only two types of coins. This allows us to buy the item, which costs 101010.
It's impossible to buy the item using only one type of coin, so the answer is 222.
Test case 222: It's impossible to buy them item even if we use all the coins we have.
